news_summary: NASA’s Roman telescope promises to uncover a vast number of previously hidden exoplanets, greatly expanding our understanding of distant worlds.
news_summary_fr: Le télescope Roman de la NASA promet de mettre au jour un grand nombre d'exoplanètes jusqu'alors inconnues, élargissant ainsi considérablement notre compréhension des mondes lointains.
news_summary_es: El telescopio Roman de la NASA promete descubrir una gran cantidad de exoplanetas hasta ahora ocultos, lo que ampliará enormemente nuestro conocimiento sobre los mundos lejanos.

articles.title: NASA’s Roman telescope could reveal 100,000 hidden worlds
articles.summary: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope could revolutionize the search for alien worlds by discovering around 100,000 exoplanets—far more than all previous missions combined. It will look deep into unexplored parts of the Milky Way, helping scientists compare planetary systems across very different galactic environments. The mission will also uncover rare Earth-sized planets, study thousands of exotic alien atmospheres, and provide a treasure trove of data that could reshape our understanding of how planets form.
articles.content:
NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is expected to dramatically expand the search for planets beyond our solar system, known as exoplanets. Scientists estimate the mission could uncover about 100,000 previously unknown worlds, a remarkable increase compared to the nearly 6,300 exoplanets discovered so far through NASA missions and other observatories.

What makes Roman especially exciting is where it will look. Most exoplanet discoveries to date have come from relatively nearby regions of the galaxy. Roman, however, will search largely unexplored areas of the Milky Way, offering a much broader view of planetary systems across our galaxy.

"Our galaxy is home to a variety of different environments, but when it comes to hunting for exoplanets, we've really only explored one: our own neighborhood," said Elisa Quintana, an exoplanet researcher at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland. Quintana leads a team focused on building software and simulations to help prepare for Roman's exoplanet transit observations. "Roman will extend the search far enough to encompass other galactic habitats, which could help us learn how planet formation varies across different regions of the Milky Way."

Today, most known exoplanets are located within a few thousand light years of Earth. One of Roman's primary surveys will look far beyond that range, examining stars through the Milky Way's densely packed central bulge and extending all the way to the far side of the galaxy.

Searching the Milky Way for New Worlds

Roman will continuously monitor stars across a large section of the Milky Way, looking for changes in their brightness.

One technique relies on planetary transits. When a planet passes in front of its star from our perspective, it blocks a small amount of starlight, causing the star to dim temporarily.

The telescope will also use a second technique called microlensing. In these events, the gravity of a foreground star and any accompanying planets magnifies the light of a more distant background star, briefly making it appear brighter.

Each method is sensitive to different kinds of planets.

The transit technique, which is expected to uncover roughly 100,000 worlds, is particularly effective at detecting large, extremely hot planets. These planets block more light from their stars and complete their orbits more frequently, making them easier to spot.

Microlensing, which is expected to reveal more than 1,000 worlds, excels at finding planets farther from their stars, including systems that resemble our own solar system. It can detect planets as small as Earth and Mars, both within habitable zones and at greater distances from their stars. Many of these worlds are extremely difficult, or even impossible, to find using other detection methods.

Together, these complementary approaches will allow scientists to investigate how planets form throughout the galaxy, including in the region where our own solar system may have originated.

Clues to Earth's Origins

Today, our solar system lies about 27,000 light years from the center of the Milky Way. Researchers believe it likely formed roughly 10,000 light years closer to the galactic center before gradually moving outward to its current location.

Evidence for this idea comes largely from the Sun's chemical composition.

Astronomers use the term heavy elements to describe all elements other than hydrogen and helium, which were produced shortly after the universe formed. Heavier elements are created inside stars and become more abundant over time as successive generations of stars live and die.

Stars located in the galaxy's outer regions generally contain fewer heavy elements. By contrast, stars in the galactic bulge are older and tend to be richer in elements such as silicon, oxygen, and magnesium.

These chemical differences may influence the types of planets that form around stars. Some systems could produce larger planets, rockier worlds, or perhaps more planets overall. In some cases, stellar composition may even affect whether planets form at all.

Astronomers have already found evidence that such relationships exist among nearby stars.

"Stars with more heavy elements tend to host more planets, especially giant ones," said Robby Wilson, a postdoctoral fellow at NASA Goddard, who led a study about Roman's expected transiting planet yield.

By examining entirely different populations of stars and planets across the Milky Way, Roman could greatly expand these studies and help reveal how common planetary systems like our own really are.

"Roman will be especially powerful because it will observe hundreds of millions of distant stars, letting scientists compare faraway planet populations to those found nearby," said Wilson. "All of that data will give us a lot to comb through, so we're prepping by creating synthetic data, detecting simulated planets, and using machine learning to filter out false positives. That way we'll be ready to go right away when real data comes pouring in."

All data collected by Roman will be publicly available, allowing researchers and citizen scientists alike to participate in the search for new worlds.

Studying Alien Atmospheres and Weather

Roman could also provide atmospheric information for thousands of the transiting planets it discovers.

"Roman won't analyze atmospheres in the same in-depth way as missions like NASA's James Webb Space Telescope, but it will gather different information on a much larger scale," Wilson said.

While the James Webb Space Telescope focuses on detailed chemical analyses of individual planets, Roman will examine broader temperature and climate patterns across thousands of worlds. This large statistical dataset could identify important trends and help guide future observations by Webb and other observatories.

One area of focus will be "hot Jupiters," giant planets roughly the size of Jupiter that orbit extremely close to their stars. Since Jupiter is about 11 times wider than Earth, these worlds are enormous and often complete an orbit in just a few days. Their high temperatures allow them to emit detectable infrared radiation.

Roman's infrared instruments will be able to observe these glowing planets and study how their brightness changes over time.

When a hot Jupiter passes in front of its star, astronomers see one dip in brightness. A second, smaller dip occurs when the planet moves behind the star and its light is temporarily blocked.

"That secondary dip tells us how bright, and therefore how hot, the planet is," said Wilson. "By tracking how the planet's brightness changes over its orbit, Roman can also see differences between the day side and night side, and even detect shifts in where the hottest region is on the planet. That tells us about atmospheric winds and heat circulation."

A New Era for Exoplanet Discovery

NASA's Kepler mission transformed exoplanet science by monitoring roughly 100,000 stars and demonstrating that planets are extraordinarily common throughout the Milky Way.

"NASA's now-retired Kepler mission's survey of 100,000 stars revolutionized the field of exoplanets over a decade ago, and taught us that planets are even more common than stars in our galaxy," said Jorge Martínez-Palomera, an astronomer at NASA Goddard who is helping prepare for Roman's exoplanet data.

Roman is expected to take that legacy much further. Its galactic bulge survey alone will observe approximately 100 million stars while exploring regions of the Milky Way that remain largely uncharted.

"Roman's galactic bulge survey will observe around 100 million stars and probe underexplored areas of our galaxy, which will provide a foundational dataset that will likewise revolutionize what we know about other worlds and our place in the Universe."
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Roman Space Telescope mission which is expected to discover around 100,000 exoplanets, vastly expanding our knowledge of planetary systems across the Milky Way. This represents a major scientific breakthrough with broad significance for humanity's understanding of the universe and planetary formation. The mission's large-scale data collection and public availability also promise to engage the scientific community and the public, making it an inspiring advancement in space exploration.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Roman Telescope set to discover 100,000 new planets in the Milky Way
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has launched the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope to explore planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets. So far, scientists have found about 6,300 exoplanets mostly close to Earth, but many parts of our galaxy remain unexplored.

**What happened** – The Roman telescope will scan large, distant areas of the Milky Way, including the crowded center and far side of the galaxy. It uses two methods: transit, which detects planets by the dimming of stars as planets pass in front, and microlensing, which finds planets by the way their gravity brightens distant stars. Roman could find about 100,000 new planets, far more than all previous missions combined.

**Impact** – This mission will give scientists a wider view of how planets form in different parts of the galaxy, including rare Earth-sized planets and giant hot planets. By studying stars with different chemical makeups, Roman will help us understand why some places create more or different kinds of planets. Its data will be open for everyone, letting researchers and the public join the search for new worlds.

**What's next step** – Roman will soon begin collecting data, and scientists are preparing by testing software and using machine learning to spot real planets and avoid mistakes. The mission’s results will guide future space telescopes in studying alien atmospheres and weather, especially on giant planets close to their stars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Roman Space Telescope will vastly increase our knowledge of planets in the Milky Way by discovering around 100,000 new worlds and revealing how planetary systems vary across the galaxy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope Roman de la NASA devrait découvrir 100 000 nouvelles planètes dans la Voie lactée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a lancé le télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman pour explorer les planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques ont découvert environ 6 300 exoplanètes, principalement proches de la Terre, mais de nombreuses régions de notre galaxie restent inexplorées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le télescope Roman va scanner de larges zones éloignées de la Voie lactée, y compris le centre dense et le côté opposé de la galaxie. Il utilise deux méthodes : le transit, qui détecte les planètes quand elles font légèrement baisser la lumière des étoiles en passant devant elles, et la microlentille gravitationnelle, qui repère les planètes grâce à la façon dont leur gravité amplifie la lumière d’étoiles plus lointaines. Roman pourrait découvrir environ 100 000 nouvelles planètes, bien plus que toutes les missions précédentes réunies.

**Impact** – Cette mission offrira aux scientifiques une vision plus large sur la formation des planètes dans différentes parties de la galaxie, y compris des planètes rares de la taille de la Terre et des géantes très chaudes. En étudiant des étoiles avec des compositions chimiques différentes, Roman aidera à comprendre pourquoi certains endroits créent plus ou différents types de planètes. Ses données seront accessibles à tous, permettant aux chercheurs et au public de participer à la recherche de nouveaux mondes.

**Prochaine étape** – Roman commencera bientôt à collecter des données, et les scientifiques préparent déjà des logiciels et utilisent l’apprentissage automatique pour détecter les vraies planètes et éviter les erreurs. Les résultats guideront les futurs télescopes spatiaux dans l’étude des atmosphères et du climat des planètes extraterrestres, en particulier des géantes proches de leurs étoiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le télescope spatial Roman de la NASA va considérablement accroître nos connaissances sur les planètes de la Voie lactée en découvrant environ 100 000 nouveaux mondes et en révélant comment les systèmes planétaires varient à travers la galaxie.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio Roman de la NASA descubrirá 100,000 nuevos planetas en la Vía Láctea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA ha lanzado el telescopio espacial Nancy Grace Roman para explorar planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas. Hasta ahora, los científicos han encontrado alrededor de 6,300 exoplanetas, principalmente cerca de la Tierra, pero muchas partes de nuestra galaxia no se han explorado.

**Qué pasó** – El telescopio Roman examinará grandes áreas distantes de la Vía Láctea, incluyendo el centro denso y el lado opuesto de la galaxia. Usará dos métodos: el tránsito, que detecta planetas cuando causan que la luz de las estrellas baje al pasar frente a ellas, y la microlente gravitacional, que encuentra planetas gracias a cómo su gravedad hace que la luz de estrellas más lejanas se vea más brillante. Roman podría descubrir cerca de 100,000 nuevos planetas, mucho más que todas las misiones anteriores juntas.

**Impacto** – Esta misión dará a los científicos una visión más amplia de cómo se forman los planetas en diferentes partes de la galaxia, incluyendo planetas raros del tamaño de la Tierra y planetas gigantes muy calientes. Al estudiar estrellas con diferentes composiciones químicas, Roman ayudará a entender por qué algunos lugares crean más o diferentes tipos de planetas. Sus datos serán públicos, permitiendo que investigadores y el público participen en la búsqueda de nuevos mundos.

**Próximo paso** – Roman comenzará pronto a recolectar datos, y los científicos preparan programas y usan aprendizaje automático para identificar planetas reales y evitar errores. Los resultados guiarán futuros telescopios espaciales para estudiar las atmósferas y el clima de planetas alienígenas, especialmente los gigantes cerca de sus estrellas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El telescopio espacial Roman de la NASA aumentará enormemente nuestro conocimiento de los planetas en la Vía Láctea al descubrir cerca de 100,000 nuevos mundos y mostrar cómo varían los sistemas planetarios en la galaxia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vast starry Milky Way galaxy viewed from a cosmic vantage point, with a glowing, intricate telescope silhouette aimed toward the dense galactic bulge where clusters of stars shimmer; around the telescope, hundreds of softly glowing orbs symbolizing newly discovered exoplanets orbit various stars, some radiating gentle infrared light to hint at hot Jupiters, all rendered in natural, muted blues, deep purples, and soft golds, evoking a sense of exploration and cosmic discovery.
